meta.title: 3.2 State Updates and Gotchas
meta.description: State does not update instantly, and updating objects and arrays has tricky rules.

blocks.prompt: If you call `setCount(count + 1)` three times in a row inside the same function, what do you think the final value of count will be? If count started at 0, would it be 1 or 3? Take a guess before reading on.

blocks.question: A component has `const [items, setItems] = useState(['a', 'b', 'c'])`. A developer writes `items.push('d'); setItems(items);` to add an item. Will the screen update to show the new item?
blocks.answer: No, or at least not reliably. `items.push('d')` mutates the existing array, and `setItems(items)` passes the same array reference back to React. React compares references and may think nothing changed, so it skips the re-render. The correct code is `setItems([...items, 'd'])` which creates a brand-new array. This is one of the most common bugs in React -- especially in AI-generated code.

problems.explanation: Both calls to `setCount(count + 1)` read the same stale value of `count`, which is 0. So both calls are `setCount(0 + 1)`, which is `setCount(1)`. The count becomes 1, not 2. To get 2, you would need functional updates: `setCount(prev => prev + 1)` for each call.

options: A) `todos.splice(index, 1)` mutates the existing array, and `setTodos(todos)` passes the same reference. React does not detect a change.
B) The `findIndex` call is incorrect and returns -1.
C) The key prop should be the index, not the id.
problems.explanation: This is the classic mutation bug. `splice` modifies the array in place, then `setTodos(todos)` passes back the same array reference. React compares the old and new references, sees they are the same object, and may skip the re-render. The fix: `setTodos(todos.filter(t => t.id !== id))` which creates a brand-new array without the deleted item.

options: A) The `setUser` call replaces the entire user object with just `{ address: {...} }`, losing `name` and `email`. It needs to spread the existing user first: `setUser({ ...user, address: { ...user.address, city: newCity } })`.
B) The onChange handler should call `setUser` directly instead of using a separate function.
C) The input value should be `user.city` not `user.address.city`.
D) useState cannot hold nested objects.
problems.explanation: When you call `setUser({ address: {...} })`, the new object only has an `address` property. The `name` and `email` properties are gone. The fix is to spread the existing user object first, then override just the nested address: `setUser({ ...user, address: { ...user.address, city: newCity } })`. You would tell the AI: "The handleCityChange function replaces the entire user object instead of updating just the city. Spread the existing user and address to preserve other fields."

blocks.prompt: Explain in your own words why `setCount(count + 1)` called three times only increments by 1. Then explain what the spread operator (`...`) does when updating an object in state and why it is necessary.

blocks.content: State updates are not instant -- the variable keeps its old value until the next render. Use functional updates (`prev => prev + 1`) when the new value depends on the old. Never mutate objects or arrays directly -- always spread to create a copy first. These two gotchas (stale closures and mutation) are the source of most state-related bugs in React.

blocks.prompt: Next time you see a bug report like "clicking the button does nothing" or "the list does not update," think about these two gotchas. Is the code reading a stale value? Is it mutating instead of copying? These are the first two things to check.

reviewQuestions.question: If you call setCount(count + 1) three times in the same function and count is 0, what is the final value?
reviewQuestions.explanation: All three calls read the same stale value of count (0), so they all compute setCount(0 + 1). The count ends up as 1, not 3. This is the stale closure gotcha.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: You remembered! The stale closure gotcha is one of the trickiest concepts, and you've got it down.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: This is the stale closure gotcha: all three calls see count as 0, so they all set it to 1. Use functional updates (prev => prev + 1) to fix it.

reviewQuestions.question: Why does items.push(newItem) followed by setItems(items) often fail to update the screen?
options: push() does not work on arrays in React
push() mutates the existing array, and React sees the same reference so it skips the re-render
reviewQuestions.explanation: push() modifies the array in place. When you pass the same array reference to setItems, React compares old and new and thinks nothing changed. Use setItems([...items, newItem]) instead.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Excellent! You understand immutability in React -- always create a new array or object instead of mutating the old one.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: push() changes the existing array without creating a new one. React checks references, sees the same array, and skips the update. The fix: setItems([...items, newItem]) to create a fresh array.

reviewQuestions.question: What does the spread operator (...) do when updating an object in state?
options: It deletes all properties from the object
It creates a copy of the object so you can override specific properties without losing the rest
reviewQuestions.explanation: The spread operator copies all properties from an existing object into a new one. You can then override specific properties: { ...user, name: 'New Name' } keeps everything except the updated name.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's right! Spread to copy, then override -- it's the standard pattern for immutable state updates.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The spread operator copies the existing object into a new one. So { ...user, name: 'New' } keeps all of user's properties but replaces name. This way React detects the change.
